cases.name: single-w2-savers-credit-50pct
cases.scenario: Single filer with modest W-2 income ($19,500) and $2,000 traditional IRA contribution. Qualifies for the 50% Saver's Credit rate (AGI ≤ $23,000 for single). Credit = 50% × $2,000 = $1,000, capped at income tax liability $375. Total tax = $0.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Retirement Savings Contributions Credit (Form 8880) module; credit rates per Rev. Proc. 2024-40, §3.43 (IRC §25B); SAVERS_CREDIT_AGI_SINGLE_2025 rate50 = $23,000

cases.name: single-w2-savers-credit-20pct
cases.scenario: Single filer with W-2 income ($24,000) and $2,000 IRA contribution qualifying for the 20% Saver's Credit rate (AGI $23,001–$25,000 for single). Credit = 20% × $2,000 = $400. Net tax = $425.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Retirement Savings Contributions Credit (Form 8880) module; credit rates per Rev. Proc. 2024-40, §3.43 (IRC §25B); SAVERS_CREDIT_AGI_SINGLE_2025 rate20 = $25,000

cases.name: single-w2-gambling-winnings
cases.scenario: Single filer with W-2 wages ($38,000) and W-2G gambling winnings ($5,000) from a casino, no federal withholding on winnings. Winnings reported on Schedule 1 Line 8b. Total income $43,000.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Other Income module (gambling winnings, W-2G); IRC §61(a)(14); IRS Pub 525; Schedule 1 Line 8b

cases.name: mfj-w2-itemized-mortgage-salt-charity
cases.scenario: Married filing jointly with two W-2 incomes ($95,000 + $55,000 = $150,000). Itemized deductions — mortgage interest $14,500, SALT $14,000 (state income tax $8,000 + real estate $6,000), cash charity $5,000. Total itemized $33,500 exceeds MFJ standard deduction $31,500.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Deductions module (Schedule A — mortgage interest Form 1098, SALT, charitable contributions); IRC §163(h), §164, §170; SALT cap = $40,000 per OBBBA §70002 (TY2025)

cases.name: single-w2-residential-clean-energy-solar
cases.scenario: Single homeowner with W-2 income ($72,000) who installed solar panels costing $20,000. Residential Clean Energy Credit (Form 5695 Part I) = 30% × $20,000 = $6,000 (nonrefundable). Excess credit carries forward.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Energy Credits module (Form 5695 Part I — Residential Clean Energy Credit, IRC §25D); IRS Pub 17 Ch. 37; 30% rate applicable TY2025

cases.name: mfj-w2-ira-deduction-savers-credit
cases.scenario: Married filing jointly with two W-2 incomes ($42,000 + $28,000 = $70,000). Taxpayer made $7,000 traditional IRA contribution (not covered by employer plan — fully deductible). Saver's Credit at 10% rate (MFJ AGI $63,000 in $50,001–$76,500 range). Credit = 10% × $2,000 = $200.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on IRA deduction (Pub 590-A, Worksheet 1-2) and Saver's Credit (Form 8880) modules; IRC §219, §25B; Rev. Proc. 2024-40 IRA_CONTRIBUTION_LIMIT_2025 = $7,000; SAVERS_CREDIT_AGI_MFJ_2025 rate10 = $76,500

cases.name: single-w2-lifetime-learning-credit
cases.scenario: Single graduate student with W-2 income ($55,000) and $10,000 qualified education expenses at an eligible institution. Lifetime Learning Credit (Form 8863) = 20% × min($10,000, $10,000) = $2,000 (nonrefundable). AGI below phase-out range ($80,000–$90,000 for single).
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Education Credits module (Form 8863 Part II — Lifetime Learning Credit); IRC §25A(c); IRS Pub 970 Ch. 3; phase-out single $80,000–$90,000 per F8863Node constants

cases.name: single-w2-foreign-tax-credit-de-minimis
cases.scenario: Single filer with W-2 wages ($68,000), foreign interest income $500 (foreign tax paid $75), and foreign qualified dividends $300 (foreign tax paid $45). Total foreign tax $120 ≤ $300 de minimis threshold for single — claimed directly on Schedule 3 Line 1 without filing Form 1116.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Foreign Tax Credit module (Schedule 3 Line 1, Form 1116 de minimis exception); IRC §901; IRS Pub 514; Form 1040 Instructions for Schedule 3, Line 1 (de minimis: $300 single / $600 MFJ)

cases.name: hoh-w2-child-dep-care-credit
cases.scenario: Head of household filer with W-2 income ($48,000) and one qualifying child age 4. Paid $3,000 in childcare to enable work. Child and Dependent Care Credit (Form 2441) = 20% × $3,000 = $600 (nonrefundable, AGI > $43,000). CTC $2,200 and dep care credit absorb all tax. ACTC = $0 (full CTC applied nonrefundably).
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Child and Dependent Care Credit module (Form 2441); IRC §21; IRS Pub 503; applicable percentage = 20% for AGI > $43,000; HOH standard deduction = $23,625 (Rev. Proc. 2024-40, OBBBA §70001)

cases.name: single-marketplace-excess-aptc-repayment
cases.scenario: Single filer with W-2 wages ($32,000) enrolled in ACA marketplace plan. SLCSP = $5,800, annual premium = $5,400, APTC advanced = $3,600. Computed PTC ≈ $3,384 (FPL ~204%, contribution ~6.3%). Excess APTC repayment = $216 added to tax liability.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Health Care Coverage module (Form 8962 — excess advance Premium Tax Credit repayment); IRC §36B; Rev. Proc. 2025-15 (PTC applicable percentages TY2025)

cases.name: mfj-w2-energy-efficient-home-improvement
cases.scenario: Married filing jointly homeowners ($85,000 + $62,000 = $147,000 wages) who installed an electric heat pump ($8,000) and new exterior windows ($2,500). Form 5695 Part II §25C: heat pump credit = $2,000 (separate §25C cap); windows credit = $600 (capped). Total §25C credit = $2,600.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Energy Credits module (Form 5695 Part II — Energy Efficient Home Improvement Credit); IRC §25C; §25C caps: windows/skylights $600, heat pumps $2,000 separate cap, overall §25C general cap $1,200/yr (heat pump is outside general cap)

cases.name: single-schedule-c-sep-ira-qbi
cases.scenario: Self-employed single filer with Schedule C net profit ($75,000). Makes $13,758 SEP-IRA contribution. SE tax deduction ($5,303.58) and SEP deduction reduce AGI. QBI deduction applies (20% of qualified business income, limited to 20% of taxable income). No withholding — amount owed.
cases.source_citation: IRS VITA Pub 4491 TY2025, illustrative case based on Self-Employment module (SEP-IRA deduction Schedule 1 Line 16, QBI deduction Form 8995); IRC §404(a)(8), §408(k), §199A; IRS Pub 560; SE tax per IRC §1401; QBI deduction per Rev. Proc. 2024-40
